scene.name: The Sounding Court, Where the Music Outlived the Players

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Pae
npcs.title: whose lullaby is still sung by people who never knew her name
npcs.hail: *(A woman sits humming by the brazier — and the humming is the first thing about her, before the face, before anything: a soft, worn, rocking little tune, the kind of melody that does not announce itself, that you feel you have heard before though you know you have not, that seems to have been worn smooth by passing through a great many mouths. She is plain, and kind-faced, and entirely ordinary, the sort of soul that no song or story would ever be written about — except that one was, in a way, though she did not know it and would be astonished to learn it. For Pae was a mother, in life, and nothing more remarkable than that; she raised her children, and she was poor, and she had no gift the world would have named a gift — except that she made up a lullaby. One night, for a fretful baby, she strung together a little tune and a few soft nonsense words, the way tired mothers do, and she sang it, and the baby slept, and she sang it again the next night, and the next, for years, to all her children, never thinking it anything but hers and theirs.)* …Oh — forgive the humming, cold one, I don't always notice I'm doing it. *(a warm, embarrassed smile)* …It's an old habit, from when the little ones were small. *(she folds her hands)* …I'm Pae. I was nobody, cold one — a mother, a poor one, in a place you've never heard of, a long time gone. I did nothing the great powers would weigh. *(she tilts her head, and something wondering crosses her plain face)* …Except they tell me — the Ushers here, the ones who *know* things — they tell me a strange thing, cold one, that I can hardly credit. …They tell me my little tune …the one I made up for a fretful baby …is *still being sung.* …Right now. …By people who never knew there was ever a Pae.*

npcs.name: Cale
npcs.title: who made one perfect thing, and never wrote it down
npcs.hail: *(A man sits with his head bowed over hands that are shaped, you can see, by a lifetime of an instrument — the calluses, the long fingers, the particular way they curl as if still holding something — and there is in him a grief so specific and so quiet that you understand it is about one thing only, held close and turned over endlessly. This is a maker of music, a true one, a composer; you can feel the gift in him the way you can feel heat off a banked fire. But the gift is shadowed by a single regret that has eaten the rest. For Cale, in life, made one thing — one piece, one work, the thing he had been reaching toward his whole life, that came to him whole and perfect in his last years the way such things sometimes come, a melody so true it seemed less invented than *found*, less written than *remembered* from somewhere realer than the world. And he played it, a few times, alone and once or twice for others. And he never wrote it down. Meaning to, always meaning to, the way one means to do the most important thing tomorrow — and then there were no more tomorrows, and Cale died with the only copy of the truest thing he ever made locked inside a soul that was about to cross over and leave the world without it.)* …A cold one. *(he looks up, and his eyes are an old grief)* …Crossed-over. Returned, they say. *(he almost laughs, bitter and soft)* …Then you can do a thing I never could, can't you. You can go *back.* …Forgive me. That's not your fault, and it's not a fair way to greet a stranger. …Sit, if you'll sit with a fool who lost the one thing he should have saved. …I'm Cale. …I made something *beautiful* once, cold one. …And I let it *die with me.* …And I have had a very long time …to learn exactly what that costs.*

npcs.name: Wisp
npcs.title: who played for no one, because the playing was enough
npcs.hail: *(Sitting on the lip of the fountain with their eyes closed and their face turned up to a sky that isn't there is a slight, weathered figure, tapping out a rhythm on their knee with two fingers and smiling at nothing — the unmistakable contentment of a soul doing, even now, even dead, the only thing it ever wanted to do. This was a street musician, you understand at once: not a great one, not a famous one, not a soul that ever stood in a hall or pleased a king, but one of the ones who sit on corners and bridge-rails and fountain-lips and simply *play*, for the coins if the coins come and for nothing if they don't, because the playing is the point and always was. They open their eyes as you approach, and the smile widens, unbothered, generous, the smile of someone who has never once in their life or death wanted anything they didn't already have.)* …Hey. *(a easy, warm voice)* …Pull up a bit of fountain. *(they pat the stone)* …I'm Wisp. I played, mostly — strings, pipes, whatever was to hand, on whatever corner had the good echo. *(they grin)* …Never made anything of it, before you ask, because everybody asks. No fame, no fortune, no great works, no name anybody'd know. Played a thousand songs to ten thousand people who walked right past, and most of 'em are gone now like smoke, every one, no record, no trace. *(they tap the rhythm, content)* …And folk get so *sad* about that, cold one, when they hear it — *'oh, all that music, just* gone, *played to nobody, lost forever, what a* waste' — *(they laugh, soft and sure)* …and I never could explain to a single one of 'em why they've got it exactly backwards. …Sit. …Maybe a Returned soul can hear it. …Why a song that's gone like smoke …was never, ever …a waste.*

variants.text: *(She makes room by the brazier, and the soft little tune keeps turning under her voice even as she speaks, an undercurrent she barely notices.)* …Sit, cold one, you're welcome by the warm. *(a gentle smile)* …I'll tell you the whole strange wonder of it, since you've a kind face and the dead have time. …I made up a lullaby once. That's all. *(she hums a phrase of it, soft)* …*Hush now, the dark's just the day's other half / sleep now, and I'll keep the lamp* — like that, cold one, a few soft words and a little rocking tune, made up tired, in the dark, for a baby that wouldn't settle. I sang it to all of mine. I never thought it was *anything* — it was just the sound of *me*, being near, in the dark, so they'd not be afraid. *(her plain face fills with wonder)* …And my children grew, cold one, and had children, and — they sang it to *theirs.* Not because I told them to. Because it was the sound of being *safe*, and you give your children the sound of being safe, it's the most natural thing there is. …And *those* children — *(she shakes her head, amazed)* …the Ushers tell me it *spread*, cold one. Mother to child, mother to child, down and down, past where anyone remembered it had a *maker* at all — until it's just — *a* lullaby, *the* lullaby, a thing people think has *always* existed, sung tonight, *right now*, in houses I'll never see, to babies whose great-great-grandmothers I never met. *(barely)* …They don't know my name, cold one. …Not one of them. …Is that — *(the wonder catches on something)* …I can't decide if that's the saddest thing I ever heard …or the *most* *wonderful.* …Maybe you can tell me which.*

nodes.speaker: Pae
nodes.text: *(She warms her hands at the brazier, and the little tune turns and turns under everything, and she gives you her gentle, ordinary, extraordinary attention.)* …Ask me what you like, cold one. *(a soft smile)* …Though I warn you, there's not much to ask about — I was a mother and I was poor and I made up one tune. That's the whole of me. …It still surprises me anyone crosses the court to talk to me at all, with the *grand* ones about.

choices.text: *(read the mother — past the ordinary, to the thing of hers still moving through the world)* "Let me look at you a moment, Pae. At the part of you that's still out there, being sung."
"Does it grieve you, Pae — that they sing your song and don't know your name? That the tune outlived the *you* of it?"

*(the one who knows a soul outlives itself in what it gave — and the Wall can't weigh a woman who survives in a tune)* "Pae — here's which it is: it's the most wonderful thing you ever heard, and I can tell you exactly why. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by what they leave that can be *held* — deeds, debts, monuments, a name carved somewhere. And it would look at you — a poor mother, no name remembered, no monument, nothing it can weigh — and call you *gone*, a soul that left no mark. And it would be more wrong about you than about almost anyone, because *you are not gone.* Every night, in houses you'll never see, a mother bends over a frightened child and sings *your* tune — your exact arrangement of safe-sounds, made by you, in the dark, out of love — and the child settles, and is not afraid, *because of a thing you made.* That's not a monument, Pae. It's better. A monument just sits there being looked at. Your song is *doing your love*, still, tonight, a thousand times over, in your absence — comforting children you'll never meet with the comfort you made. You didn't leave a mark, Pae. You left a *living thing*, and it's still doing the work you made it for. The Wall can't weigh a woman who's still, right now, singing a thousand babies to sleep. Your name being forgotten isn't the song forgetting *you* — it's the song carrying the only part of you that ever mattered so far and so wide that it stopped needing your name to keep doing your love."
"Sing me the whole of it, Pae. The lullaby. I'd like to carry the sound of it, even if I already half-know it somehow."
"Keep humming, Pae. I'll come back — I like the tune; it's older than you know."

nodes.speaker: Pae
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, a little shy of being the object of such attention, and you let your cold move past the plain ordinary woman to the strange and luminous truth of her.)* *(And your sense finds something it has never quite found before: a soul that is only partly here. Most souls are wholly present, all of themselves gathered in the one place; but your sense reads, in Pae, a self that is *distributed* — that most of her is here, ordinary and kind by the brazier, but that a real and living piece of her is *elsewhere*, scattered across the living world, present in every mouth that sings her tune. Your sense reads the lullaby and reads that it is not merely a thing she made; it is a thing she put herself *into*, a vessel that carries, faithfully, the exact quality of one particular mother's love — the specific tenderness, the specific reassurance, the specific message *you are safe, the dark is only the day's other half, I am here* — and that this cargo did not fade or distort as the song passed down the generations but arrived intact, so that a child sung to sleep tonight, ten lifetimes removed, is receiving, unknowing, the actual love of a poor woman dead a long age. And your sense reads the wonder the Ushers tried to tell her: that this is a kind of life after death that has nothing to do with the afterlife at all, a continued existence in the living world through the made thing, and that Pae is, in the most literal sense your cold can read, still *working* — still comforting, still keeping the lamp, still doing in the world the one loving act she made the song to do. Your sense reads that the forgetting of her name is real but does not touch this; the name was only ever the label, and the love was the cargo, and the song carried the cargo and dropped the label as unnecessary weight, because a frightened child does not need to know the name of the woman whose love is settling it — it needs only the settling, and the settling arrives, faithfully, every night, made by Pae, delivered by the song, ten thousand times over, world without end. Your sense reads, last, that she is one of the least-mourned and most-alive souls in all the doors: a woman the great powers would weigh as nothing, who is, demonstrably, still loving the living, by the thousand, tonight.)*

nodes.speaker: Pae
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a moment, turning her wedding-worn hands toward the brazier's warmth, and the little tune pauses, for once, as she thinks.)* …Does it grieve me. *(softly)* …I thought it would, cold one, when the Ushers first told me. My first feeling was a kind of — *ache.* That all those souls have my *song* on their lips and not one of them knows there was ever a *me*, a Pae, a woman who sat in the dark and *made* it. It felt like being — *robbed*, almost. Like the song had *left* me behind. *(she shakes her head slowly)* …But then I thought about *why* I made it, cold one. And it set the ache right down. *(she meets your eyes)* …I didn't make that tune so that people would *remember Pae.* I never once, in all the nights I sang it, thought *'this should make me famous, this should carry my name.'* …I made it so my *babies* wouldn't be *afraid* of the *dark.* *That* was the whole of the wanting. *(her plain face fills)* …And *that* — cold one — that the song *does.* *Still.* For *every* baby it reaches. The thing I *made* it for, it is *doing*, tonight, a thousandfold. *(softly)* …So why would I grieve that it dropped my *name* along the way? My name was never the *point.* The *not-being-afraid* was the point. …And if my little tune had to *choose*, cold one — between *carrying Pae's name* to a handful of souls who'd think *'how nice, a woman named Pae made this'* … …or *carrying the not-being-afraid* to *thousands* who'll never know my name but will *sleep* *safe* because of it — *(she smiles, certain, at peace)* …*God*, cold one. …I'd drop the name *every* time. …It was *never* about being *remembered.* …It was about the *babies* not being *scared.* …And *that* the song *remembered* *perfectly* …long after it *forgot* *me.*

nodes.speaker: Pae
nodes.text: *(Her face lights, and she does not need asking twice — a mother always has the lullaby ready — and she sits up, and folds her hands, and sings it for you, the whole of it, soft and worn and true.)* *(And it is nothing, and it is everything. A simple rocking melody, a handful of phrases, the kind of tune you could teach a child in an afternoon: hush now, the dark's just the day's other half; sleep now, and I'll keep the lamp; the cold can't come where the song is warm; I'm here, I'm here, till the morning comes. That is all it is. There is no cleverness in it, no art that a master would praise, nothing a great composer could not have written in a careless minute.)* *(And your cold, listening, understands why it conquered the world. Because it was never trying to be clever. Every note of it is bent to one purpose only — to be the sound of a loving adult, near, in the dark, saying you are safe — and it achieves that purpose so completely, so without distraction or ego or ornament, that it bypasses the ear entirely and goes straight to the oldest, most frightened place in any soul, the place that was once small and afraid of the dark, and it settles that place, the way it has settled a hundred generations of small frightened places, because it is, quite simply, the most concentrated dose of pure reassurance ever folded into a minute of sound.)* *(Pae finishes, and looks at you, a little shy.)* …That's it. That's the whole of it. …Silly, isn't it? …Such a *little* thing. …*(quietly)* …But you felt it, didn't you, cold one. …Even *you.* …Even *dead.* …The *not-being-afraid.* …It still *works.* …It always *works.* …That's the only clever thing about it: …it *only* ever tried to do *one* thing …and it does that *one* thing …*perfectly.**

nodes.speaker: Pae
nodes.text: *(She listens, and the little tune stops entirely, and slow tears stand in her plain kind eyes, and when you finish she presses her warm-from-the-brazier hands to her face and laughs, wet and wondering.)* …*'Still doing your love, in your absence.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …I have felt *foolish*, since the Ushers told me — *foolish* for being a *nobody* who somehow *spilled* into the *world*, like it was an *accident*, like I *snuck* a bit of myself into history by *luck.* *(she shakes her head)* …And you've — you've made it not an accident. You've made it the *truest* thing I ever did. *(her voice fills)* …I'm not *gone.* …I kept *thinking* of it as the song *leaving* me behind — and you've turned it *round* — the song didn't *leave* me, cold one, the song is *carrying* me, the *best* of me, the *loving-in-the-dark* part, *out* past where my *body* or my *name* could *ever* have *gone.* *(fiercely gentle, through tears)* …And your *Wall.* *(she lowers her hands, and her plain face is luminous)* …Your Wall would weigh *me* as *nothing* — no monument, no name, no mark it can *hold.* …But it's *holding* the *wrong* *things*, isn't it, cold one. …It weighs what *sits* *still* and can be *counted.* …And the *most* of *worth* …*doesn't* sit *still.* …It *moves.* It *passes* *hand* to *hand*, *mouth* to *mouth*, *mother* to *child*, *doing* its *work* in *the* *living* *world* …*long* after the *one* who *made* it is *dust* and the *name* is *lost.* *(she meets your eyes)* …*Go* and *tell* your Wall, cold one: …you cannot *weigh* a *soul* by what it *left* *behind* …because the *realest* *souls* …*didn't* *leave* anything *behind.* …They *sent* the *best* of *themselves* *on* *ahead*, *into* the *living*, …and they're *out* *there* *still* — *(barely)* …*singing.* …Now go. …And — cold one? …If you ever, out there, in the *living* *world*, hear a mother singing a baby down with *'hush now, the dark's just the day's other half'* … …*(she smiles, radiant)* …that's *me.* …Still *here.* …Still keeping the *lamp.* …*Nod* to me, would you? …I'll be *there.**

nodes.speaker: Pae
nodes.text: *(She nods, and the little tune starts up again under her breath, automatic as a heartbeat, as you rise.)* …Go on, cold one. Mind the others. *(she nods across the court)* …There's a man yonder with a deep grief — *Cale* — a *true* maker, a *real* one, not a *fumbling* mother with one lucky tune like *me.* *(her face softens)* …He made something *grand*, cold one, and he *lost* it — let it *die* with him, never wrote it down — and he's been *eating* himself over it an *age.* *(quietly)* …Be *gentle* with Cale. His is the *opposite* of *my* trouble: *I* made a *little* thing and it *outlived* me past all reason; *he* made a *great* thing and it *died* in his *arms.* …And the *bright* one on the fountain — *Wisp.* *(she smiles)* …*Wisp* will tell you music that's *gone* like *smoke* was never *wasted*, and *Wisp* is *right*, and it's a thing *worth* *hearing.* …Between the *three* of us, cold one — the *song* that *lasted*, the *song* that was *lost*, and the *song* that was *never* *meant* to *last* — *(she meets your eyes)* …I think we're the *whole* *answer* …to whether a *beautiful* thing *has* to *endure* …to have been *worth* the *making.* …And the answer, cold one — *(she smiles, and resumes her humming)* …the answer is *no.* …*Go* and hear it …from all *three* of *us.**

variants.text: *(He looks up at you and the old grief in his face has been transfigured — not erased, but lifted, like a held breath finally let go — because you carry, now, the thing he thought had died forever, and he can hear, in the way you hold yourself, that it is safe.)* …You still have it. *(barely)* …My — the piece. I can see it on you, cold one, the way I'd see a man carrying my own child. *(his composer's hands open, trembling)* …An age, I've grieved it. The truest thing I ever made, gone, locked in a dead man, never written, never to be — and you *learned* it. You took it *into* you. And you're going to carry it *back*, to where the *living* are, where it can be *played* again, *heard* again, *written* down at last by someone who — *(his voice breaks)* …it's not lost. *(he presses his hands to his face)* …After everything, after all my grieving — it turns out the thing I should have done was never *write it down.* It was *give* it to someone who could *carry* it. …And the gods sent me a soul that walks *between* the *worlds.* …Thank you, cold one. …You've done the thing I failed to do. …You *saved* it. …*Go* and *set* it *loose.**

*(He gestures, tiredly, to the log beside him, his shaped hands curling as if around an instrument that isn't there.)* …Sit, cold one. I'll not pretend to be good company — I've a grief that's worn a groove in me, and it shows. *(a bitter, soft breath)* …I'm Cale. A composer. A *good* one — I'll not be falsely modest, false modesty is just vanity with better manners — I had a *real* gift, and I gave my whole life to it, and I made, in my last years, the *one* thing I'd been reaching for since I was a boy. *(his face fills with an old wonder, then grief)* …It came to me *whole*, cold one. A melody so *true* it didn't feel *invented* — it felt *found*, like I'd *overheard* it from somewhere realer than the world, and only had to *write* it *down.* *(his hands clench)* …And I *didn't.* …I *meant* to. Always *meant* to — *tomorrow*, *next* week, when I had the *good* paper, when the *light* was better — the way you put off the most important thing precisely *because* it's the most important and you want to do it *right.* *(barely)* …And then I *died*, cold one. …With the truest thing I ever made …still *unwritten* …*locked* in here — *(he touches his chest)* …and it *crossed* over *with* me …and the *world* …*never* *got* it. …*That's* me, cold one. …The man who *made* something *beautiful* …and let it *die* in his *arms* …for want of *picking* up a *pen.**

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(He folds his shaped hands and waits, with the heavy stillness of a man who has told himself the same grief so many times he no longer expects it to change.)* …Ask, if you like. *(a tired look)* …Though I warn you, cold one, every road in me leads back to the same place — the pen I didn't pick up. I'm poor company for a soul on a journey. …But ask. It's been a long while since anyone wanted to.

choices.text: *(read the lost maker — past the grief, to whether the beauty truly died, or only the record of it)* "Let me look at you a moment, Cale. At the thing you think you lost."
"Tell me about it, Cale. The piece. The truest thing you ever made. Even if it only lives in you now — let me hear what it was."

*(offer to carry it — learn the melody faithfully, take it back to the living where it can live again)* "Cale — stop grieving and listen, because I can do the one thing you couldn't. I walk between the worlds. I'm going *back* — to where the living are, where there's paper and pens and players and ears. Play it for me. Teach it to me, note by note, until I have it whole and true. And I'll carry it out of here, in me, the way you should have carried it onto a page — and I'll find someone, a player, a maker, and I'll give it to them, and it will be *written down at last*, and *heard*, and it will *live.* You don't need a pen anymore, Cale. You need a soul that can carry it past death's door. And that's exactly, precisely, the one thing I am. Teach me your song."

*(the one who knows a beauty witnessed even once is not nothing — and the Wall can't weigh what changed a soul invisibly)* "Cale — even if I couldn't carry it, you need to hear this. You say you let it die, that the world never got it. But you played it — a few times, you said, alone and once or twice for others. Which means it was *heard.* Souls *witnessed* it. And a beauty that true doesn't pass through a soul and leave nothing — it *changes* the one who hears it, in some small permanent way they can't even name, and they carry that change forward into everything they do after, and it touches others, invisibly, untraceably, forever. The pen would have let it be *repeated.* But even unrepeated, even once, it *landed*, and it *worked*, and it is *out there still*, not as notes on a page but as the altered shape of every soul that ever heard you play it. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs what can be counted and kept. It can't weigh a beauty that existed for one evening and changed everyone in the room forever. That's not nothing, Cale. That's the realest kind of something there is — a thing that left no record and changed the world anyway."
"Hold the song safe a little longer, Cale. I'll come back — and I think I've a way to carry it."

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(He lets you look, too tired to guard himself, and you let your cold move past the worn grief to the thing he carries locked inside.)* *(And your sense finds it — the piece — and understands at once both why he grieves and why the grief is not quite the whole truth. The melody is *there*, intact, held perfect in the soul that made it, and your sense reads it and aches, because it is exactly as true as he believes: not clever, not ornamented, but *true*, a sequence of sound that seems to describe something the heart has always known and never had words for, the kind of beauty that does not decorate the world but briefly *explains* it. Your sense reads that he did not lie or exaggerate; he made a genuinely great thing. And your sense reads the grief, and finds it is the specific grief of the *maker* rather than the *lover* of beauty: that what tortures Cale is not that the beauty is *gone* — it is not gone, it is right here, he can play it in his mind whenever he likes — but that it is *unshared*, that the bridge between his soul and the world, the written page, was never built, and so the most precious thing he ever made is trapped on the wrong side of death with no way across. And your sense reads the thing Cale cannot see past his grief: that the bridge he mourns — the pen, the page — was never the only bridge. That a melody held perfect and whole in a soul can be *taught*, *passed*, *carried*, mouth to ear to memory, the way Pae's lullaby crossed ten generations with no page at all; that the written record is only *one* way a song survives, and not even the oldest or the truest one. And your sense reads, last, the cruel and hopeful irony at the heart of him: that Cale spent his grief believing the lesson was *I should have written it down* — when the real lesson, the one that could free him, is that he should have *sung it to someone who would carry it on.* The page was never the point. The *passing* was the point. And a soul that walks between the worlds is the one bridge that can still be built.)*

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(Something shifts in him — the grief making room, for a moment, for the thing under it, which is love — and he closes his eyes, and his shaped hands lift, unconsciously, into the playing-shape.)* …What it was. *(softly)* …I'll never make you *hear* it, cold one, not in words — that's the whole *cruelty* of music, it lives in a country words can't enter — but I'll tell you what it was *for*, what it *did*, and maybe that's nearer. *(his hands move, slow, in the air)* …You know the feeling, cold one, when something is *ending* — a good day, a life, a love — and you feel two things *at once* that *shouldn't* fit together: the *grief* that it's *over*, and the *gratitude* that it *happened*? The way those two press against each other so hard you can't *breathe*? *(barely)* …I spent my whole life trying to make a *sound* that was *both* of those at once. The grief and the gratitude. The *ending* and the *worth-it.* …Most music picks *one* — it's *sad*, or it's *glad.* …I wanted the *one* that was the *whole* *truth*: that the *sweetness* and the *sorrow* of a thing are *not* *opposites*, that they are the *same* *note*, *struck* *together.* *(his hands still)* …And in my last years, cold one, it *came* to me. Whole. The sound of *a beautiful thing ending and being glad it existed.* …I played it, and I *wept*, not from *sorrow* and not from *joy* but from *both*, *exactly* *both*, *perfectly* *balanced* — *(his voice fails)* …and I thought, *'there. that's it. that's the thing I was born to make. I'll write it tomorrow.'* …*That's* the piece, cold one. …A sound that holds the whole *truth* about *endings.* …And — *(he laughs, broken)* …and isn't it *exactly* like the *gods* …that the man who made the *truest* *song* about *loss* …*lost* it. …I *became* my own *song*, cold one. …The beautiful thing …that *ended.**

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(You don't just offer — you sit, and you listen, and you give yourself wholly to the learning, humming it back phrase by phrase, correcting, again, until you have it not approximately but *exactly*, every interval, every breath, the soul of it and not just the shape — and a man who thought he would carry the truest thing he ever made into oblivion watches it pass, living, into another soul, and it is the single most beautiful thing that has happened to him in life or death.)* *(He plays it for you, all of it, his hands shaping the air, his cracked voice carrying the line — the sound of a beautiful thing ending and being glad it existed — and you take it in, and you give it back, and his face when he hears his own song returned to him whole, alive, *safe*, in another's keeping, is the face of a soul set free.)* …you have it. *(he is weeping, openly, without grief)* …you have it *exactly.* not *near* — *exactly* — the *turn* in the third phrase that everyone *flattens*, you kept it *sharp*, the way it *has* to be, the way it *hurts* *right* — *(he grips your hands)* …cold one. an *age* I have *grieved* this, and the *whole* time the *answer* wasn't *paper*, it was *you* — a soul that could *take* it *up* and *walk* it *out* the *one* *door* I couldn't. *(fiercely, luminous)* …*carry* it. *guard* it. and when you reach the *living* — find a *player*, a *true* one, and *give* it to them, and tell them — *(his voice fills)* …tell them it's called *The Glad of It.* tell them a *dead* man made it and a *dead* man *carried* it and it's *theirs* now, to *play* and *pass* on, *forever.* *(barely)* …and *now*, cold one — *now* I can *rest*, because the thing I *failed* to do, *you've* *done* — you've built the *bridge* I was too *slow* to build — and my *truest* *song* …is going to *outlive* me after *all.* …go. …*set* it *loose* in the *living* *world.* …and your *Wall*, cold one — *(fierce)* …tell it a *beauty* it can't *weigh* …just walked *out* of *death* …*singing.**

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(You offer to carry it, and you mean it, and you set yourself to learn — and though you are no master, you are *earnest*, and you get most of it, the shape and the heart if not every perfect interval, and Cale watches his song pass into another soul with a wonder that eases, by a real degree, the grief of an age.)* …you have — *(he listens, intent)* …you have the *most* of it, cold one. the *bones.* the *shape.* the *turn* is a little — here, it's *this* — *(he corrects you, gently, and you take it)* …better. *(he sits back, and something in him loosens)* …it's not *perfect*, cold one — I'll be honest, a master would hear the *seams* — but it's *true* *enough*, and *true* *enough* is *infinitely* more than *gone*, which is what it was a *moment* *ago.* *(barely)* …you'd really *carry* it? out, to the living? *(the grief lifts, a fraction)* …then come *back*, cold one, and let me *teach* it you *again*, and *again*, until the *seams* are *gone* and you have it *whole* — because a thing *worth* *carrying* is *worth* carrying *right.* *(a tired, real, dawning hope)* …I thought the song *died* with me. …And you're telling me it only needs a *better* *student* and a *few* more *lessons* …and it *lives.* …*come* *back*, cold one. …I have an *age* of *teaching* in me …and *finally* …*finally* …someone *worth* teaching it *to.**

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(You offer to carry it — but there's a haste in you, an eagerness to *have* it, to *get* it and go, and a maker who spent a lifetime on a single true thing hears the rush and flinches.)* …Wait. *(he holds up a shaped hand)* …You're — cold one, you're *reaching* for it like a thing to be *grabbed.* *Quick.* *Got* and *gone.* *(quietly)* …And that's — that's not how a song is *carried*, cold one. A song isn't *taken.* It's *learned*, and learning it *true* is *slow*, it's *humble*, you have to *sit* with it and let it *teach* *you*, note by note, until you don't just *know* it, you *love* it the way *I* love it — because a song carried *without* love arrives *dead*, all the *notes* and *none* of the *soul.* *(he lowers his hand)* …I *believe* you mean to help, cold one. But you're trying to *carry* my *child* by *grabbing* its *arm.* *(barely)* …Come *back* when you've the *patience* to *learn* it *slow* — to let it *matter* to you the way it *mattered* to me. …I *waited* an *age*; I'd *rather* wait *longer* …than send my *truest* thing into the *world* *carried* *carelessly.* …It deserves a *bearer* …who'll carry it like it's *worth* *something.* …*Be* that, cold one. …And *then* I'll *give* it you.*

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong — in your eagerness you hum back a careless approximation, breezy, *near enough*, flattening the very turn that is the soul of the thing, and treating the holy specific truth of it like a tune to be whistled — and Cale's face goes white, and he pulls the song back from you as a man snatches a child from the edge of a well.)* …*No.* *(stricken)* …No — you've — that's *not* it, cold one, you've *flattened* the *turn*, you've made it — you've made it *pretty*, *ordinary*, a *tune*, and it is *not* a *tune*, it is the *one* *true* *thing* and you've just — *(he presses his hands to his face)* …you'd *carry* it like *that?* *Approximate?* *Near* *enough?* …Do you understand, cold one, that a song carried *wrong* is *worse* than a song *lost?* …A *lost* song is *mourned*, *whole*, *true*, in the *grieving* of it. …A song carried *wrong* is *replaced* — the *false* version goes out into the *world* and *crowds* *out* the *true* one *forever*, and people sing the *flattened* thing and think *that's* what it *was*, and the *real* one dies *anyway*, only now there's a *counterfeit* *wearing* its *name.* *(he turns away, shaking)* …I would *rather* it *die* with *me*, *true*, than walk the *world* *false.* …*Go.* …And do *not* come *back* to *carry* it …until you *understand* that *'near* *enough'* …is the *one* thing a *true* song *can* *not* *survive.* …*Learn* it *exactly* …or *leave* it *with* me …to die *whole.* …Those are the *only* *two* *mercies* …it has *left.**

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the worn grief in his face slowly, slowly changes — not lifting all the way, but shifting, as a thing reconsidered from a new angle changes shape — and when you finish he is quiet a long moment, and then he lets out a breath that seems to carry an age of self-reproach out with it.)* …*'A beauty that existed for one evening and changed everyone in the room forever.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …I have *counted* the *playings*, you know. *Obsessively.* *'Only* *four* *times.* *Only* *four* *rooms* *heard* it before I *let* it *die.'* …As though *four* were a *failure*, a *pittance*, *nothing* against the *thousands* it *should* have reached. *(he shakes his head slowly)* …And you've — you've made me *count* it *differently.* *Four* *rooms.* …But every *soul* in those *four* *rooms*, cold one — *(his voice fills)* …I *saw* their *faces.* I *saw* the thing the *song* *did* to them — that *opening*, that *both-at-once*, the *grief* and the *gratitude* *struck* *together* — I *saw* it *land*, and I *saw* them *changed* by it, and then they *walked* *out* into their *lives* *carrying* that *change*, and they were *kinder*, maybe, or *braver*, or just *less* *alone* in their *own* *endings* — *(barely)* …and *that* went *on*, didn't it. *Untraceable.* *Forever.* …The *song* wasn't *written* *down.* …But it was *written* *into* *them.* *(and the old maker lifts his head, and there is something almost like peace)* …And your *Wall*, cold one — *(fierce now)* …your *Wall* would *weigh* my *life's* *work* as *zero* — *'no* *score*, *no* *page*, *nothing* it can *hold.'* …But it *can't* *see* the *four* *rooms*, can it. …It *can't* *weigh* the *change* in a *soul* that *can't* be *counted* …*rippling* *out* through *every* life it *touched* *after.* *(he meets your eyes)* …*Tell* it, cold one: …the *realest* beauties …*leave* *no* *record.* …They don't *survive* on *pages.* …They *survive* in the *altered* *shape* …of *everyone* who *witnessed* them. …And *that*, your *Wall* …*can* *not* …*weigh.**

nodes.speaker: Cale
nodes.text: *(He nods, and his shaped hands settle, and for the first time since you came there is something other than grief in the set of them — a fragile, dawning hope.)* …Go, cold one. *(quietly)* …And — come *back.* If you *meant* it — about *carrying* it — *(he can hardly say it)* …then come back and *learn* it from me, *slow*, and *true*, and I will *give* you the *one* thing I *have* left, the *truest* thing I ever *made*, to *carry* out the *door* I *cannot* *walk.* *(he glances across the court)* …And while you've a moment — see the others. *Pae*, by the brazier — her trouble is the *mirror* of mine, cold one: she made a *little* thing and it *outlived* her past all *reason*, where I made a *great* thing and *lost* it. *(a soft, rueful breath)* …And *Wisp*, on the fountain. *(something easing in him)* …*Wisp* will tell you a thing I *needed* to hear my whole *life* and *never* did: that a song *gone* like *smoke*, *played* to *no* one, *lost* the *moment* it's *made* — was *never* a *waste.* *(barely)* …I built my whole *grief*, cold one, on the *opposite* — on *'it must be* saved, *it must* last, *it must* be *written* or it's *worthless.'* …*Wisp* knows *better.* …*Go* and let *Wisp* *teach* you …the thing that might have *spared* me …an *age* of *grieving* a *song* …that was *never*, for *one* *moment* …*wasted.**

conversations.title: Wisp, Who Played for No One

variants.text: *(They shift along the fountain's lip to make room, easy and unhurried, and the two-finger rhythm never stops tapping out its quiet patient pulse on their knee.)* …There you go. Comfy? Good. *(a warm grin)* …So you want to know the thing folk always get backwards. I can tell — you've got the look. Everybody gets the look when they hear how I spent it: corners and bridges and fountain-lips, a thousand songs to people walking past, no fame, no fortune, nothing *saved*, nothing *kept*, and then *dead*, and *all* of it just — *gone.* *(they spread their hands, smiling)* …And the *look*, cold one, the *sad* one you're trying not to do right now — it says *'what a* waste. *all that music, played to the* wind, *lost* *forever.'* *(they laugh, gentle)* …Here's the thing I could never make a *living* soul understand, cold one, because the living are *so* *afraid* of things *ending* that they can't *hear* it. …I *knew.* The *whole* time. I knew *every* song I played was *gone* the *moment* I played it — *gone* like a *breath*, like a *wave*, like the *warm* of a *fire* you'll *never* feel *again.* *(they tap the rhythm, perfectly content)* …I *knew* it was all *smoke.* …And I played it *anyway*, *every* day, with my *whole* *heart* …*because* it was *smoke.* …*(they grin)* …Sit, cold one. …Let me tell you the *secret* the *savers* and the *keepers* never learn: …*the* *song* *that's* *gone* …was *the* *most* *alive* *one* *there* *ever* *was.**

nodes.speaker: Wisp
nodes.text: *(They keep tapping the rhythm, eyes half-closed, the very picture of a soul that has everything it wants and wants nothing it doesn't have.)* …Ask away, cold one. *(an easy smile)* …I've all the time there is and nothing to guard. A soul that never *kept* anything hasn't got much to *hide*, turns out. It's restful. You should try it.

choices.text: *(read the player — past the ease, to whether the contentment is real or a brave face on a wasted gift)* "Let me look at you a moment, Wisp. At whether all that peace is the true thing."
"Why play for no one, Wisp? A thousand songs to people walking past, nothing kept. Truly — why pour a life into smoke?"

*(the one who knows the Wall can't weigh the ephemeral — that a thing needn't last to have been worth making)* "Wisp — you're the answer to a question the whole rest of this court is asking, and I think you're the one who's got it right. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by what *lasts* — what endures, what's saved, what can be pointed to after you're gone. And by that measure you're the emptiest soul in all the doors: nothing saved, nothing kept, a thousand songs gone like breath. But here's what the Wall, and the savers, and the keepers all miss: *a thing doesn't have to last to have been worth making.* A sunset doesn't last. A wave doesn't last. The warmth of a fire, a laugh, a kiss, a single perfect afternoon — none of it lasts, and not one soul who ever lived would call those things *wasted* for ending. You knew, every day, that you were making something that would be gone in a breath — and you made it anyway, fully, beautifully, because the *being* of a thing was always the point, not the *lasting* of it. The Wall measures permanence and calls it worth. And you spent a whole life proving the most alive things in all creation are exactly the ones that *don't* last — that are precious *because* they're passing, that you give your whole heart to *knowing* they're smoke. You're not the emptiest soul here, Wisp. You're the one who understood that worth was never about lasting. It was about *being*, fully, while you are."
"There's a man across the court, Wisp — Cale — eaten alive because he made one great thing and let it die unsaved. You're his cure, aren't you? What would you tell him?"
"Keep playing, Wisp. I'll come back — smoke or not, I like the sound."

nodes.speaker: Wisp
nodes.text: *(They let you look, entirely unbothered, still tapping the rhythm, and you let your cold move past the easy contentment to see whether it goes all the way down — bracing, from habit, for the wasted-gift grief beneath.)* *(And there isn't one. That is the quiet revelation: the peace is real, bedrock, total, with no buried regret beneath it at all. Your sense reads a soul that made, early and completely, a peace that most souls never manage even at the end — peace with *impermanence*, with the passing of things, with the fact that everything beautiful is also leaving. Your sense reads that Wisp did not arrive at this peace through loss or hardship forcing resignation; they *chose* it, young, as a way of seeing, and reads the choice: that Wisp looked at the world and saw that everything in it — every sunset, every song, every living moment — was already, even as it happened, *passing*, and that a soul has only two options in the face of that, to *grieve* the passing or to *love* it, and that grieving it makes you clutch and hoard and fear, while loving it makes you *generous*, *present*, *free.* And your sense reads what that choice made of Wisp's music: that because they never tried to *keep* a song, never recorded or hoarded or sought to make any of it *last*, every single song they ever played was given *completely*, held back *nothing* for the future, spent *entirely* in the moment of playing — and your sense reads that this is *why* the music was so alive, why people who walked past on a thousand corners *felt* something even as they kept walking, because they were hearing a thing made with the whole heart and *no* part of it reserved against tomorrow. Your sense reads that the savers and keepers, who hold back, who make for posterity, who play to *last* — they always, always, reserve a little, keep a little safe, and the kept-back part is the part the listener can feel is *missing.* Wisp kept nothing back. Every song was the last song, played like the last song, because every song *was* — gone in a breath, and so given without reservation. And your sense reads, last, the deep truth Wisp built a life on: that the things that don't last are not the lesser things. They are the *most* alive things, precisely because they are passing, and a soul brave enough to love what's leaving — instead of grieving it or hoarding against it — gets to live, every single day, in the company of beauty, fully, freely, asking nothing of it but that it *be*, briefly, and then graciously letting it go.)*

nodes.speaker: Wisp
nodes.text: *(They smile, and tip their head back toward the absent sky, and the rhythm goes on, unhurried.)* …Why play for no one. *(warmly)* …Cold one, I'll let you in on it: I *wasn't* playing for *no one.* That's the *savers'* word for it, *'no one'* — they mean *no one who'd* *remember*, *no one who'd make me* *famous*, *no audience that* *lasted.* *(they grin)* …But there was *always* someone. The *person* *walking* *past.* The one who *didn't* *stop*, *didn't* *pay*, *didn't* *clap* — but who, for the *three* *steps* it took to *cross* the *reach* of my *song*, *heard* it, and felt their *day* go a *half-shade* *warmer*, and walked *on*, never *knowing* *why* they felt a *little* less *alone* at the *corner* of *such-and-such* *street.* *(softly)* …*That* was my audience, cold one. The *passing* *stranger.* The *three-step* *listener.* And do you know — *(they sit forward, the one moment of intensity)* …there is *no* *purer* *audience* in all the *world*, cold one, than a soul who hears your song and *keeps* *walking.* …Because they *can't* *give* you *anything.* No *coin*, no *fame*, no *memory* — they'll *forget* it by the *next* *corner.* …Which means when I played for *them*, cold one, I was playing for *nothing* *back.* *Pure.* *Just* the *song*, *given*, to a *stranger*, for the *half-shade* of *warmth* it'd *put* in a *day* I'd *never* *hear* *the* *end* *of.* *(they lean back, content)* …The *savers* play for *posterity* — for a *future* that'll *applaud* them when they're *gone.* …I played for the *only* thing that's *real*, cold one: …*this* *corner*, *this* *stranger*, *this* *breath.* …And *that*, it turns out …is *enough.* …It was *always* *enough.* …I never *once* …went *home* *empty.**

nodes.speaker: Wisp
nodes.text: *(Their easy face goes gentle, and they glance across the court at the bowed grieving man, and the rhythm slows, tender.)* …Ah. *Cale.* *(softly)* …Yeah. I know Cale's trouble, cold one. We've talked, him and me — well, *I've* talked; he mostly *grieves*, but he *listens*, under it. *(they nod)* …And you're right. I'm — not his *cure*, that's too grand, but I'm the thing he *couldn't* let himself *believe.* *(they set their hands still, for once)* …Here's what I'd tell him, cold one, what I *have* told him, what he can't *quite* *hear* yet through the *grief*: …*Cale, the song didn't fail because it wasn't saved. The song* *succeeded* *the moment you played it true, even once, even to four rooms. It* *happened.* *It was* *real.* *It* *landed.* *And then it was* *gone* *— like every real thing — and the gone-ness isn't the* *failure*, *it's the* *nature* *of it.* (their voice goes warm and certain)* …He thinks he had a *beauty* and *lost* it, cold one. …I'd tell him: *you didn't* lose *it. You* *spent* *it.* *You played the truest thing you ever made* to *living* *souls who* *felt* *it* — *that's not a* loss, *Cale, that's the whole* *point*, *that's what music is* *FOR* — *and the only reason you call it a tragedy is that you measured it by the savers' ruler, the one that says a thing's only worth keeping if it* *lasts.* (softly)* …But the *lasting* was *never* the *worth*, cold one. …*Cale* made a *real* thing and *gave* it to *real* *souls* in *real* *rooms* …and then, like *all* real things, it *passed.* …And he's spent an *age* *grieving* the *passing* …when he should be *proud* of the *playing.* *(they meet your eyes)* …*That's* what I'd tell him. …*'You didn't lose your song, Cale. You* *played* *it. And the playing was the having. There was never going to be more than that — for* *any* *of us.'*

nodes.speaker: Wisp
nodes.text: *(They listen, and for once the easy grin goes still, and something moves behind their content eyes that is deeper than contentment — the look of a soul that has known a true thing all alone for a very long time and is, at last, hearing it spoken back.)* …*'The most alive things are exactly the ones that don't last.'* *(softly)* …Cold one. …I have *known* that my *whole* *life* and *most* of my *death*, and I have *never*, *not* *once*, heard another soul *say* it back to me. *(they laugh, quiet and amazed)* …They all think I'm — *simple.* *Content* because I never *wanted* *more.* A *happy* *little* *busker* who didn't *know* enough to *grieve* what he *threw* *away.* *(they shake their head)* …And you've *named* it: I didn't *throw* anything *away.* I *loved* what was *leaving* — *on* *purpose* — *because* it was *leaving* — and that's not *simple*, cold one, that's the *hardest* *thing* a *soul* can *learn*: to *open* your *hands* and *love* a thing *fully* *while* *knowing* you *can't* *keep* it. *(fiercely gentle)* …And your *Wall.* *(they sit forward, intent)* …Oh, your *Wall* is the *greatest* *saver* of *all*, isn't it, cold one. The *greatest* *keeper.* It *weighs* what *lasts*, *hoards* what *endures*, *measures* a *soul* by the *monument* it *leaves* — and so it's *blind*, *completely* *blind*, to the *whole* *kingdom* of *worth* that *lives* in the *passing*: the *sunset*, the *wave*, the *song* gone like *smoke*, the *single* *afternoon* you'd *not* *trade* for an *eternity* of *lesser* *ones.* *(they grin, fierce and free)* …*Tell* your *Wall*, cold one: …*you* *cannot* *weigh* a *life* by what it *kept* …because the *best* of *living* …*can't* be *kept.* …It can *only* be *spent*, *fully*, in the *breath* it *happens* …and then *let* *go.* *(they lean back, and pick the rhythm back up, content and luminous)* …*Go* on, cold one. …Go *spend* yourself at that *Wall* …like a *song* you *know* is *smoke.* …*Hold* *nothing* *back* for *posterity.* …*That's* the only way *anything's* …*ever* *worth* *playing.**

nodes.speaker: Wisp
nodes.text: *(They give you a warm, lazy salute and tip their face back to the absent sky, the rhythm tapping on.)* …Go well, cold one. *(a grin)* …Mind the other two before you go — they're the *book-ends* of me, those two. *Pae*, by the brazier — her little song *lasted*, past all reason, and she's *lovely* about it, but it was *luck*, cold one, *grace*, not something she *aimed* at. *(they nod)* …And *Cale* — *(their face softens)* …*Cale* made the *great* thing and it *didn't* last, and he's *breaking* on it. …Go *help* Cale, cold one, if you can. *(quietly)* …I've *told* him the thing — *'the playing was the having'* — but he *can't* hear it from *me*, because I'm the *busker*, the *simple* one, *'what would* *Wisp* *know* about *losing* a *masterwork.'* *(barely)* …But *you* — a soul that walks *between* the *worlds* — *you* might *reach* him. …And here's a *busker's* *tip*, cold one, *free*: …if you can *carry* his *song* *out* — *take* it, *learn* it, *walk* it back to the *living* — *do* it. …Not because it *needs* to *last.* *Nothing* needs to *last.* …But because *Cale* needs to *learn*, before he can *rest*, that his song was *worth* *playing* *whether* or *not* it *lasts* — …and *one* good way to *teach* a *saver* that …*(they grin)* …is to *save* the *thing* *for* him …so he can *finally* *stop* *needing* it *saved.* …*Go.* …And *play* it *loud*, wherever you take it. …*Smoke* deserves to be *loud.**
